title: Tab
summary: Tabs allow users to navigate between groups of related content at the same level of hierarchy.
preamble: The tab component organises content across different screens, data sets, and other interactions. It makes it easy to explore and switch between different views without leaving the current page.
anatomy-overview: Tab consists of a tab bar with individual tab items. Each tab item contains a label and optionally a leading icon. The active tab is visually distinguished from inactive tabs.
anatomy-details:
1.  Tab bar
    
2.  Tab item
    
3.  Tab label
    
4.  Active indicator
    
5.  Leading icon (optional)

overview.title: Default
overview.section-content: Tabs are used as local navigation to organise related content into logical groupings. Users can switch between views without losing the context of the page.

overview.title: Overflow behaviour
overview.section-content: When tabs exceed the available width, all tabs that cannot fit are grouped into a list in a fold-out modal. On mobile with only two tabs, each tab takes up 50% of the width.

overview.title: Tabs vs segmented control
overview.section-content: Tabs and segmented controls can look similar, but they serve very different purposes. Choosing the wrong component leads to user confusion and misaligned expectations.

columns.title: Use tabs when
columns.content:
Use tabs to navigate between **completely different views or distinct sections of content**. Each tab reveals unique content that is unrelated to what the other tabs show.

Think of tabs as separate binders — switching tabs takes the user to an entirely different set of content.

*   The content under each tab is meaningfully different in nature
    
*   Users are unlikely to need to compare the contents of multiple tabs at once
    
*   The tabs act as primary or secondary navigation within the page
    
*   Content groupings are based on different topics, data sets, or tasks

columns.title: Use segmented control when
columns.content:
Use segmented control to **filter or toggle the presentation of the same underlying content**. The data does not change — only how it is displayed or sorted.

Think of segmented control as a switch that changes the lens through which you view the same document.

*   You are switching between different representations of the same data (e.g. list vs. grid, weekly vs. monthly)
    
*   The options act as filters or view toggles within a single context
    
*   The component is embedded within a form or a content area, not used as page navigation

dolist.text: Use tabs for navigating between accounts (Savings, Loans, Funds) — each contains entirely different content.
Use segmented control to switch between a list view and a chart view of the same transaction data.
Use segmented control to toggle between time periods (Week, Month, Year) on the same graph.

dontlist.text: Don't use tabs to toggle between views of the same data set — this is the role of segmented control.
Don't use segmented control as page-level navigation between unrelated sections.
Don't mix up the two components — tabs are for navigation, segmented control is for filtering or view switching.

overview.title: Guidelines
overview.section-content: Tabs are best suited for a small number of clearly distinct content groupings. Order tabs according to user needs, placing the most commonly accessed tab first.

dolist.text: Use tabs to navigate between groups of content.
Keep the number of tabs low, preferably fewer than four.
Order tabs according to user needs — the most commonly used tab should come first.
Align tab content to the left with the first tab.
Use tabs when users will not need to view all sections at once.

dontlist.text: Don't use tabs to toggle data or parts of a form — use segmented control for this.
Don't add sub-headings under tab labels.
Don't use tabs if the user needs to read all sections to complete a task.

overview.title: Disabled tabs
overview.section-content: Individual tabs can be disabled to indicate that a section is unavailable. Use this sparingly and only when the reason for unavailability is clear to the user.

dolist.text: Disable a tab only when the section is temporarily unavailable and will become accessible again — for example, due to missing permissions or incomplete preconditions.
Provide context elsewhere on the page explaining why a tab is disabled and how the user can enable it.

dontlist.text: Don't disable a tab permanently — remove it from the tab bar instead.
Don't disable a tab without giving the user a way to understand why or how to proceed.

overview.title: Link tabs
overview.section-content: Tabs can render as anchor links, navigating to a new URL rather than switching panel content inline. Use link tabs for top-level or cross-page navigation where each tab corresponds to a distinct URL.

dolist.text: Use link tabs when each tab maps to a unique URL and the browser should handle navigation.
Use link tabs for primary or secondary page-level navigation sections.

dontlist.text: Don't use link tabs when switching between inline panels — use standard tabs instead.
Don't mix link tabs and panel tabs within the same tab bar.

overview.title: Leading and trailing content
overview.section-content: Tab items support a leading slot for icons and a trailing slot for supplementary content such as notification badges or counts. Use these slots to aid recognition or communicate status — not for decoration.

dolist.text: Use a leading icon to reinforce the meaning of a tab label when the icon adds clarity.
Use a trailing badge or count to communicate a quantity or status relevant to the tab's content — for example, the number of unread notifications.

dontlist.text: Don't use a leading icon purely for decoration — every icon should carry meaning.
Don't use trailing content in a way that makes the tab label hard to read or the tab bar feel cluttered.

ux-text.title: Writing UX text for tabs
ux-text.section-content:
Tab labels serve as both navigation menu items and implicit sub-headings for the content they reveal. This means labels must be clear and descriptive enough to stand on their own — without requiring a sub-heading beneath them.

Do not capitalise the first letter of every word in tab labels. Use uppercase only for:

*   The first letter of the first word
    
*   Proper names
    
*   Abbreviations

ux-text.title: Tab labels
ux-text.section-content: Because tab labels also act as sub-headings, they must be explicit and self-explanatory. Avoid vague or generic labels that do not describe the content.

ux-text.title: Common tab labels in overviews
ux-text.section-content:
Use consistent and recognisable labels for common content groupings. Standard tab labels used in overviews include:

*   **Value** – presents details about the current holding
    
*   **Payments** – shows upcoming, pending, and historical payments
    
*   **About the account** – shows details about the account or service, such as connected services, interest rates, and fees
    

When applicable, include the subject area in the label, for example _About the loan_ or _Fund transactions_.
